full_transcript.text: If there is dark matter out there, you're hoping it's actually somehow detectable.

full_transcript.speaker: Don Lincoln
full_transcript.text: I mean, I don't know what it is. I think it's cool. It's very, very fascinating. That is one thing I really do hope in my lifetime is... is understood, because I'd like to know the answer to that.

full_transcript.speaker: Lex Fridman
full_transcript.text: And that's a thing that you could, there's legitimately you can see a discovery of.

full_transcript.speaker: Don Lincoln
full_transcript.text: You gotta get lucky though. I mean, you gotta look in the right place, whatever it is.

full_transcript.speaker: Lex Fridman
full_transcript.text: Just imagine.

full_transcript.speaker: Don Lincoln
full_transcript.text: Or you have to come up with that really cool theoretical idea that everybody's overlooked, which is another possibility. And there are people who are really, religiously hating dark matter, largely because we've looked so hard for so many years and the experiments in today's world are a million times more sensitive than when I was a... a starting student and they still haven't seen anything. And that's why people really hate dark matter, I mean some of them, because they think we should have seen it by now. But, you know, I don't know.

full_transcript.speaker: Lex Fridman
full_transcript.text: I mean I'm a sucker for direct observation. Not... indirect is obviously also really great, but direct. Just imagine pointing your telescope in a certain direction and because of some...

full_transcript.speaker: Lex Fridman
full_transcript.text: artifact of cosmology being able to directly detect a giant amount of a thing that you could say is dark matter.

full_transcript.speaker: Don Lincoln
full_transcript.text: Yeah, you would see it orbit, things orbit it, or it would eclipse things in front of it, or...

full_transcript.speaker: Lex Fridman
full_transcript.text: Yeah, like in an obvious way. Because some of the stuff you mentioned with the DF2 and DF4, those are like brilliant indirect deductions that there should be something like dark matter. But some obvious, yeah, blocking, occluding this kind of thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Don Lincoln
full_transcript.text: We did that in the 90s, experiments called MACHO, OGLE and some others. They looked for a black hole that you just can't see. You know, a black hole you can't see, it's perfect, it's a perfect candidate for dark matter. And if there's enough of them out there, remember there's five times the number of stars, which means there's a whole lot of freaking black holes out there, we should have seen them and we didn't.

full_transcript.speaker: Lex Fridman
full_transcript.text: What a grand mystery. We've covered so many of them. I could talk to you for a thousand more hours, Don. Let me if I can ask you about a little bit more of a on the personal side. You have a really inspiring life story. Your folks didn't go to college. Can you just tell me about your childhood and where you found the love for physics and science and maybe how you found your journey to become a physicist given the context of where you came from?

full_transcript.speaker: Don Lincoln
full_transcript.text: Well, you know, I grew up a poor kid in the boondocks. Great parents, but not ones that could guide me terribly academically, but very, very nurturing. You know, my mom would laugh that she could stop helping me math after like sixth or seventh grade, you know. But they were supportive and there were a couple of things that, couple three things I think that folded into it. One is I was a voracious reader as a kid. I loved science fiction, I would read a book a day. It drove my mother nuts because she would try to be nice, she'd buy me a book and I'd say thank you and then next day it'd be done, you know, and just drove her completely nuts. But anyways, science fiction is good for fostering imagination and so that's precisely what it did. In addition, and this is where the more serious science came along, there were lovely science communicators that were popular in the 1970s, Isaac Asimov, Carl Sagan, guy by the name of George Gamow, they wrote books about science aimed at a layperson. I was a kid, I surely couldn't read a textbook and understand it, but I could read and and you know get a hint of what science was. And on top of that, you know, I was as most scientist, people who became scientists, irrepressibly...

full_transcript.speaker: Don Lincoln
full_transcript.text: curious about everything. Um, and I had sort of a quasi-philosophical mind. I mean, I was interested in things that, questions that have in the past been theological and then philosophical and now are more scientific. Questions about how did the universe come into existence? Um, why is the universe the way it is? Why are the laws of the universe what we see them to be? How will the universe, was it created? How will it be destroyed? These are, you know, big questions that have bothered humanity for, well, thousands of years.

full_transcript.speaker: Don Lincoln
full_transcript.text: And so, you know, I did, you said I had, um, you know, philosophy and religion minors in college and I did because I was curious about that. Um, I was hoping that learning that history might help me understand these questions. Um, and it was in college where I came to realize that the answers that I were searching for were not to be found in those directions, but I still learned about how those questions have been asked in the past.

full_transcript.speaker: Don Lincoln
full_transcript.text: And so I became a scientist. And the only question was was I going to be a cosmologist/astrophysicist or a particle physicist? And then when I had to make that decision, it was the mid 80s, and at the time there were a lot fewer cosmology measurements. There was an awful lot of thinking about the universe and not enough measuring. Whereas with particle physics, by god you could do experiments. And so the what attracted me was the ability to actually get an answer and not just mull over what an answer might be.

full_transcript.speaker: Don Lincoln
full_transcript.text: And so I became a particle physicist. Um, it was difficult without having, you know, family mentors or anything like that, but but you know, I managed. And that actually is why, well I'm here and why I've spent a fair bit of my time writing books and so forth, because I figure that there has to be some other kid out there in Iowa or Kansas, Montana, somewhere out in some little town without a lot of access to the kinds of things that people, you know, who have highly educated parents do.

full_transcript.speaker: Don Lincoln
full_transcript.text: And I'm hoping that, you know, some of them will have read some of the things I've written and will find their own path forward, because I found it very rewarding over the years. And um, you know, I've been doing this long enough that I'm I'm sure this is true. I've had kids come up to me at the lab and say, hey, I'm a summer intern because I saw your video or read your book or, you know, whatever. Um, so I know that at least I've made a small impact. I mean, I always would like to do more and, you know, I appreciate the opportunity that your audience affords me, because I I think it's important to talk about these things. These are really...

full_transcript.speaker: Don Lincoln
full_transcript.text: Cool, fascinating questions, they are unanswered and they are just waiting for youngsters to come and spend some time thinking about them because one of your viewers might be one of the people who answer these questions that have stymied very smart people for decades.

full_transcript.speaker: Lex Fridman
full_transcript.text: And we should also say that you're a legit scientist, so what we'll mention Sean Carroll, who's a legit scientist, legit physicist, but is also a good science communicator. Anyway, I just wanted to mention, I don't know if this is true, but I kind of heard you talk about this, that when you first showed up to Fermi lab, you were like working crazy hours, working extremely hard, 8:00 a.m. to midnight. First of all, I love that. Can you speak to what drove you and maybe the value of hard work in those contexts, in your in the early career when you discover a thing you're passionate about?

full_transcript.speaker: Don Lincoln
full_transcript.text: Well, yeah, I mean, obviously being smart, you know, if you're Einstein then maybe you can slack, I guess, although even he didn't do that. But I'm not Einstein. But the fact is when I was young and I was unencumbered, no family, no kids or something, I couldn't imagine anything I wanted to do more. I mean, some people, they want to go out to the club, they want to, I don't know, play soccer or something, but I wanted to make measurements and I wanted to understand and learn. And that was fantastic. And so as a graduate student, and this isn't for everybody, but I worked outrageously. I would, from Monday through Saturday, I would be at the lab voluntarily because I wanted to be from 8:00 a.m. to midnight. And on Sunday, I would work from 8:00 until about 5:00, and that's because from 5:00 to midnight, I had to wash clothes and buy groceries and things like that. And I loved it, you know? And I still love it. I can't do that anymore, but that's simply because I have other obligations. But had I been rich, I would have done the same thing. You know, it's something I truly, truly loved. And the, I mean, there is absolutely nothing more fascinating to me than having a hard problem and figuring it out. And that, you know, that work ethic, well, there's a couple of things that separate smart people from no-kidding scientists, because all scientists are smart. But the thing that separates, that many scientists have, is a drive and a real grit. The, for me and for so many scientists that I know, trying to measure something and having it not work just kind of ticks me off. And I am not going to let the universe in my lab or whatever beat me.

full_transcript.speaker: Don Lincoln
full_transcript.text: Some people say, you know, if the thing breaks, it's like, oh man, that didn't work. And a lot of people, well, I'm going to go home, I'm fed up. Nah, it would just kind of make me mad, and I'd put more effort into it. And, you know, not everyone, okay, I was crazy, I worked long hours, but I think the people who are really good at this will do maybe not that much, you know, some people have to have a better life than that. But, but a lot, because it's just, you can't imagine not knowing the answer. And that, if when you see that, as an older guy, you don't maybe not to that degree, but when you see that kind of drive, that, that intensity of trying to get the answers, you know that person's a winner. And so, if, you know, some student out there, if it doesn't, you know, bring you joy, as that what's her name, the Japanese gal says, if it doesn't bring you joy, then it might not be for you. And then you could be a person who reads about it, and, you know, is involved, but if you want to be a real scientist, it, it has to be just part of what you are here.

full_transcript.speaker: Lex Fridman
full_transcript.text: And by the way, it is a hard life, but it is also a very fulfilling one. So working hard towards this thing you love is a really fulfilling way to, to be.

full_transcript.speaker: Don Lincoln
full_transcript.text: I think that's true for an artist or something, you know, anybody, a musician, you know, musician, they just keep practicing because it is who they are.

full_transcript.speaker: Lex Fridman
full_transcript.text: Well, I'm glad there's people like you at a place I admire like Fermilab. One of the many places in the United States and the world that is carrying the beacon of great science and great engineering forward. Uh, Don, thank you so much for everything you do, for all the teaching you do online, for all the incredible physics works that you do at Fermilab, and, uh, thank you so much for talking today.

full_transcript.speaker: Don Lincoln
full_transcript.text: Well, thank you for having me.

full_transcript.speaker: Lex Fridman
full_transcript.text: Thanks for listening to this conversation with Don Lincoln. To support this podcast, please check out our sponsors in the description where you can also find links to contact me, ask questions, give feedback, and so on. And now let me leave you with some words from Marie Curie, a two-time Nobel Prize winner, first in physics, second in chemistry. Nothing in life is to be feared. It is only to be understood. Thank you for listening, and I hope to see you next time.

highlights: The history of physics is defined by a quest for unification, demonstrating that seemingly unrelated phenomena—such as a falling apple and orbiting planets, or a lightning bolt and a refrigerator magnet—are governed by the same underlying laws.

Particle physicist Don Lincoln expresses skepticism toward a "Theory of Everything," arguing that it is mathematically arrogant to believe our current theories can accurately predict the laws of nature at energy scales a quadrillion times higher than what we can experimentally measure.

A profound mystery in cosmology is why the universe is made of matter; since matter and antimatter are created in equal amounts from energy, our existence suggests a tiny, one-in-a-billion asymmetry occurred shortly after the Big Bang.

Dark energy represents what is often called "the worst prediction in physics" because there is a staggering discrepancy of 120 orders of magnitude between the observed expansion of the universe and the vacuum energy density calculated by quantum field theory.

Beyond the "aha" moments of genius, scientific progress relies on a "combative, jerky critique" where ideas are subjected to rigorous, often brutal validation through experimentation to ensure they reflect reality rather than just elegant mathematics.
summary:
In this conversation, Lex Fridman and particle physicist Don Lincoln explore the history of physics through the lens of "unification"—the centuries-long quest to prove that the diverse phenomena of the universe are governed by a single set of underlying principles. Lincoln traces this trajectory from Isaac Newton’s unification of terrestrial and celestial gravity to James Clerk Maxwell’s merging of electricity and magnetism. A central theme is the development of the Standard Model and the 2012 discovery of the Higgs boson, which Lincoln describes as a "punctuation point" that validated how fundamental particles acquire mass through the Higgs field.

The discussion shifts to the current frontiers of physics, focusing on the massive mysteries of dark matter and dark energy. Lincoln provides a pragmatic, experimentalist’s perspective on these phenomena, arguing that while we have strong indirect evidence for their existence—such as the rotation of galaxies and the accelerating expansion of the universe—their true nature remains unknown. He expresses skepticism toward the immediate success of a "Theory of Everything" or String Theory, noting the staggering energy gap between current particle accelerators and the Planck scale. He suggests that we are likely centuries away from a final unification, as we lack the "lever arm" to predict reality at such extreme scales.

Throughout the transcript, Lincoln emphasizes the necessity of the scientific method, where "jerky critique" and experimental validation must temper creative theoretical sparks. He shares his personal journey from a curious child in a non-academic household to a senior scientist at Fermilab, highlighting the value of "grit" and persistence. The conversation concludes with a focus on the next generation of scientists, with Lincoln expressing hope that current mysteries like neutrino oscillations and matter-antimatter asymmetry will eventually be solved by those driven by the same irrepressible curiosity that fueled the giants of the past.
